The **University of Virginia** (**UVA** or **Virginia**) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university#United_States "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") located in [Charlottesville City](/wiki/Charlottesville,_Virginia "Charlottesville, Virginia") and [Albemarle County](/wiki/Albemarle_County,_Virginia "Albemarle County, Virginia") in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), United States. It was founded in 1819 by [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and contains his [Academical Village](/wiki/The_Lawn "The Lawn"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Its original governing Board of Visitors included three [U.S. presidents](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States "List of presidents of the United States"): Jefferson, [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), and [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe"), the latter as sitting president of the United States at the time of its foundation. As its first two [rectors](/wiki/Rector_(academia)#United_States "Rector (academia)"), Presidents Jefferson and Madison played key roles in the university's foundation, with Jefferson designing both the [original courses](#1800s) of study and the university's [original architecture](#Academical_Village). The original campus contains President Monroe's [former residence and law office](/wiki/Brown_College_at_Monroe_Hill "Brown College at Monroe Hill"), today used as a [residential college](/wiki/Residential_college "Residential college").

Now expanded to 1,135 acres (459 ha) of central campus, the modern university is composed of eight undergraduate and three professional schools: the [School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Law "University of Virginia School of Law"), the [Darden School of Business](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_Darden_School_of_Business "University of Virginia Darden School of Business"), and the [School of Medicine](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Medicine "University of Virginia School of Medicine").[[11]](#cite_note-Depts-13) The university has been a member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") since 1904.

The university's leadership, faculty, research staff, and alumni have included several [United States presidents](/wiki/United_States_president "United States president"), foreign [heads of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), [Nobel laureates](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Wikidata:University_of_Virginia/Listeria/UVa_people/Nobel_laureates "wikidata:Wikidata:University of Virginia/Listeria/UVa people/Nobel laureates"), [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners, [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"), [Fulbright Scholars](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program"), and 57 [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship")—the most of any state university. Its alumni include 31 state governors (including fourteen [Governors of Virginia](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Virginia "List of governors of Virginia")) and 33 [United States senators](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). UVA students and alumni have founded companies such as [Reddit](/wiki/Reddit "Reddit"), [Skillshare](/wiki/Skillshare "Skillshare"), [VMware](/wiki/VMware "VMware"), and [Space Adventures](/wiki/Space_Adventures "Space Adventures").

Its athletic teams, the [Virginia Cavaliers](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers "Virginia Cavaliers"), have twice won the [Capital One Cup](/wiki/Capital_One_Cup "Capital One Cup") for overall athletics prowess and compete in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") as members of the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of Virginia](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_Virginia "History of the University of Virginia")

### 1800s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1800s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Gilbert_Stuart_Thomas_Jefferson.jpg/250px-Gilbert_Stuart_Thomas_Jefferson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gilbert_Stuart_Thomas_Jefferson.jpg)

[Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"), the university's founder, by [Gilbert Stuart](/wiki/Gilbert_Stuart "Gilbert Stuart") (c. 1821)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/University_of_Virginia_Rotunda_in_2006.jpg/250px-University_of_Virginia_Rotunda_in_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Virginia_Rotunda_in_2006.jpg)

[The Rotunda](/wiki/The_Rotunda_(University_of_Virginia) "The Rotunda (University of Virginia)"), as pictured from the South Lawn

In 1802, while serving as president of the United States, [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") wrote to artist [Charles Willson Peale](/wiki/Charles_Willson_Peale "Charles Willson Peale") that his concept of the new university would be "on the most extensive and liberal scale that our circumstances would call for and our faculties meet," and it might even attract talented students from "other states to come, and drink of the cup of knowledge."[[12]](#cite_note-Peale-14) Virginia was already home to the [College of William & Mary](/wiki/College_of_William_%26_Mary "College of William & Mary") in Williamsburg, but Jefferson lost all confidence in his *alma mater,* partly because of its religious nature—it required all its students to recite a [catechism](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism")—and its stifling of the sciences.[[13]](#cite_note-yalereview1878-15)[[14]](#cite_note-russell-16) Jefferson had flourished under [William & Mary](/wiki/College_of_William_%26_Mary "College of William & Mary") professors [William Small](/wiki/William_Small "William Small") and [George Wythe](/wiki/George_Wythe "George Wythe") decades earlier, but the college was in a period of great decline and his concern became so dire by 1800 that he expressed to British chemist [Joseph Priestley](/wiki/Joseph_Priestley "Joseph Priestley"), "we have in that State, a college just well enough endowed to draw out the miserable existence to which a miserable constitution has doomed it." These words would ring true some seventy years later when William & Mary fell bankrupt after the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and the Williamsburg college was shuttered completely in 1881, later being revived as primarily a small college for teachers until it regained university status later in the twentieth century.[[15]](#cite_note-17) Jefferson envisioned his new university would "be based on the illimitable freedom of the human mind. For here we are not afraid to follow truth wherever it may lead, nor to tolerate any error so long as reason is left free to combat it."[[16]](#cite_note-Reason-18)

In 1817, three presidents (Jefferson, [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe"), and [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison")) and [Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States_Supreme_Court "Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court") [John Marshall](/wiki/John_Marshall "John Marshall") joined 24 other dignitaries at a meeting held in the Mountain Top Tavern at [Rockfish Gap](/wiki/Rockfish_Gap "Rockfish Gap"). After some deliberation, they selected nearby Charlottesville as the site of the new University of Virginia.[[17]](#cite_note-19) The UVA Board of Visitors purchased just outside [Charlottesville](/wiki/Charlottesville,_Virginia "Charlottesville, Virginia") a farm that had once been owned by James Monroe.[[18]](#cite_note-20) The Commonwealth of Virginia chartered a new flagship university to be based on the site in Charlottesville on January 25, 1819.

[John Hartwell Cocke](/wiki/John_Hartwell_Cocke "John Hartwell Cocke") collaborated with James Madison, Monroe, and Joseph Carrington Cabell to fulfill Jefferson's dream to establish the university. Cocke and Jefferson were appointed to the building committee to supervise the construction.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The UVA Office of Equal Opportunity and Civil Rights is continuing to "seek opportunities to engage and acknowledge with respect that we live, learn, and work on [what once was] the territory of the [Monacan Indian Nation](/wiki/Monacan_Indian_Nation "Monacan Indian Nation")."[[20]](#cite_note-22) Like many of its peers,[[21]](#cite_note-23) the university owned slaves who helped build the campus.[[22]](#cite_note-apmreportsshackledlegacy-24) They also served students and professors.[[22]](#cite_note-apmreportsshackledlegacy-24) The [Memorial to Enslaved Laborers](/wiki/Memorial_to_Enslaved_Laborers "Memorial to Enslaved Laborers") on Central Grounds was built to memorialize their lives and legacies.[[23]](#cite_note-25) The university's first classes met on March 7, 1825.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

In contrast to other universities of the day, at which one could study in either medicine, law, or divinity, the first students at the University of Virginia could study in one or several of eight independent schools – medicine, law, mathematics, chemistry, ancient languages, modern languages, natural philosophy, and moral philosophy.[[25]](#cite_note-27) Another innovation of the new university was that higher education would be separated from religious doctrine. UVA had no divinity school, was established independently of any religious sect, and [the Grounds](#Campus) were planned and centered upon a library, [the Rotunda](/wiki/Rotunda_(University_of_Virginia) "Rotunda (University of Virginia)"), rather than a church, distinguishing it from peer universities still primarily functioning as seminaries for one particular strain of Protestantism or another.[[26]](#cite_note-sem-28) Jefferson opined to philosopher [Thomas Cooper](/wiki/Thomas_Cooper_(American_politician,_born_1759) "Thomas Cooper (American politician, born 1759)") that "a professorship of theology should have no place in our institution",[[27]](#cite_note-29) and never has there been one. There were initially two degrees awarded by the university: *Graduate,* to a student who had completed the courses of one school; and *Doctor* to a graduate in more than one school who had shown research prowess.[[28]](#cite_note-1911EB-30)

Jefferson was intimately involved in the university to the end, hosting Sunday dinners at his [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello "Monticello") home for faculty and students. Jefferson viewed the university's foundation as having such great importance and potential that he counted it among his greatest accomplishments and insisted his grave mention only his status as author of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and [Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom](/wiki/Virginia_Statute_for_Religious_Freedom "Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom"), and father of the University of Virginia. Thus, he eschewed mention of his national accomplishments, such as the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") and any other aspects of his presidency, in favor of his role with the young university.

Initially, some of the students arriving at the university matched the then-common picture of college students: wealthy, spoiled aristocrats with a sense of privilege which often led to brawling, or worse. This was a source of frustration for Jefferson, who assembled the students during the school's first year, on October 3, 1825, to criticize such behavior; but was too overcome to speak. He later spoke of this moment as "the most painful event" of his life.[[29]](#cite_note-bb-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/James_Madison.jpg/250px-James_Madison.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Madison.jpg)

[James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") was the second rector of the University of Virginia until 1836.

Although the frequency of such irresponsible behavior dropped after Jefferson's expression of concern, it did not die away completely. Like many universities and colleges, it experienced periodic [student riots](/wiki/Student_riots "Student riots"), culminating in the shooting death of Professor [John A. G. Davis](/wiki/John_A._G._Davis "John A. G. Davis"), Chairman of the Faculty, in 1840. This event, in conjunction with [the new UVA Honor System](/wiki/Honor_system_at_the_University_of_Virginia "Honor system at the University of Virginia") and the growing popularity of temperance and a rise in religious affiliation in society in general, seems to have resulted in a permanent change in student attitudes toward reporting the bad behavior, and thus such behavior among students that had so greatly bothered Jefferson finally vanished.[[29]](#cite_note-bb-31)

In the year of Jefferson's death in 1826, poet [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") enrolled at the university, where he excelled in Latin.[[30]](#cite_note-32) The [Raven Society](/wiki/Raven_Society "Raven Society"), an organization named after Poe's most famous poem, continues to maintain 13 West Range, the room Poe inhabited during the single semester he attended the university.[[31]](#cite_note-33) He left because of financial difficulties. The [School of Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "University of Virginia School of Engineering and Applied Science") opened in 1836, making UVA the first comprehensive university to open an engineering school.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/John_Hartwell_Cocke_cropped.png/250px-John_Hartwell_Cocke_cropped.png)](/wiki/File:John_Hartwell_Cocke_cropped.png)

J. Hartwell Cocke supervised construction with Jefferson.

Unlike the majority of Southern colleges, the university was kept open throughout the Civil War, despite its state seeing more bloodshed than any other and the near 100% [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") of the [American South](/wiki/American_South "American South").[[32]](#cite_note-CivilWarTrust-34) After [Jubal Early](/wiki/Jubal_Early "Jubal Early")'s total loss at the [Battle of Waynesboro](/wiki/Battle_of_Waynesboro,_Virginia "Battle of Waynesboro, Virginia"), Charlottesville was willingly surrendered to Union forces to avoid mass bloodshed, and UVA faculty convinced [George Armstrong Custer](/wiki/George_Armstrong_Custer "George Armstrong Custer") to preserve Jefferson's university.[[33]](#cite_note-35) Although [Union troops](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army") camped on the Lawn and damaged many of the Pavilions, Custer's men left four days later without bloodshed and the university was able to return to its educational mission. However, an extremely high number of officers of both Confederacy and Union were alumni.[[34]](#cite_note-1913alumninews-36) UVA produced 1,481 officers in the [Confederate Army](/wiki/Confederate_Army "Confederate Army") alone, including four major-generals, twenty-one brigadier-generals, and sixty-seven colonels from ten different states.[[34]](#cite_note-1913alumninews-36) [John S. Mosby](/wiki/John_S._Mosby "John S. Mosby"), the infamous "Gray Ghost" and commander of the lightning-fast [43rd Battalion Virginia Cavalry](/wiki/43rd_Battalion_Virginia_Cavalry "43rd Battalion Virginia Cavalry") ranger unit, had also been a UVA student.

Thanks to a grant from the Commonwealth of Virginia, tuition became free for all Virginians in the year 1875.[[35]](#cite_note-AmCyc-37) During this period, the University of Virginia remained unique in that it had no president and mandated no [core curriculum](/wiki/Core_curriculum "Core curriculum") from its students, who often studied in and took degrees from more than one school.[[35]](#cite_note-AmCyc-37) However, the university was also experiencing growing pains. As the original [Rotunda](/wiki/The_Rotunda_(University_of_Virginia) "The Rotunda (University of Virginia)") caught fire and [was gutted](#Academical_Village) in 1895, there would soon be sweeping changes, much greater than merely reconstructing the Rotunda in 1899.

### 1900s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1900s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Alderman_1920.jpg/250px-Alderman_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alderman_1920.jpg)

[Edwin Alderman](/wiki/Edwin_Alderman "Edwin Alderman") was UVA's first president between 1904 and 1931 and instituted many reforms toward modernization.

Jefferson had originally decided the University of Virginia would have no serving president. Rather, this power was to be shared by a rector and the [Board of Visitors](/wiki/Board_of_Visitors "Board of Visitors"). But as the 19th century waned, it became obvious this cumbersome arrangement was incapable of adequately handling the many administrative and fundraising tasks of the growing university.[[36]](#cite_note-EVAlderman-38) [Edwin Alderman](/wiki/Edwin_Alderman "Edwin Alderman"), who had only recently moved from his post as president of [UNC-Chapel Hill](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill") since 1896 to become president of [Tulane University](/wiki/Tulane_University "Tulane University") in 1900, accepted an offer as president of the University of Virginia in 1904. His appointment was not without controversy, and national media such as *[Popular Science](/wiki/Popular_Science "Popular Science")* lamented the end of one of the things that made UVA unique among universities.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

Alderman stayed 27 years, and became known as a prolific fund-raiser, a well-known orator, and a close adviser to U.S. president and UVA alumnus [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson").[[36]](#cite_note-EVAlderman-38) He added significantly to the University Hospital to support new sickbeds and public health research, and helped create departments of geology and forestry, the [School of Education and Human Development](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Education_and_Human_Development "University of Virginia School of Education and Human Development") (originally the Curry School of Education), the [McIntire School of Commerce](/wiki/McIntire_School_of_Commerce "McIntire School of Commerce"), and the summer school programs in which young [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe") took part.[[38]](#cite_note-40) Perhaps his greatest ambition was the funding and construction of a library on a scale of millions of books, much larger than the Rotunda could bear. Delayed by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), Alderman Library was named in his honor in 1938. Alderman, who seven years earlier had died in office en route to giving a public speech at the [University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana%E2%80%93Champaign "University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign"), is still the longest-tenured president of the university.

In 1904, UVA became the first university south of Washington, D.C. to be elected to the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"). After a gift by [Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie") in 1909 the University of Virginia was organized into twenty-six departments across six schools including the [Andrew Carnegie School of Engineering](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "University of Virginia School of Engineering and Applied Science"), the [James Madison School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Law "University of Virginia School of Law"), the [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe") School of International Law, the [James Wilson](/wiki/James_Southall_Wilson "James Southall Wilson") School of Political Economy, the [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") School of English and the [Walter Reed School of Pathology](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_Medical_School "University of Virginia Medical School").[[28]](#cite_note-1911EB-30) The honorific historical names for these schools – several of which have remained as modern [schools of the university](#Organization_and_administration) – are no longer used.

In December 1953, the University of Virginia joined the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") for athletics. At the time, UVA had a football program that had just broken through to be nationally ranked in 1950, 1951, and 1952, and consistently beat its rivals North Carolina and Virginia Tech by scores such as 34–7 and 44–0. Other sports were very competitive as well. However, the administration of [Colgate Darden](/wiki/Colgate_Darden "Colgate Darden") de-emphasized athletics, defunding the department and declining to join the ACC before being overruled by the Board of Visitors on that decision. It would take until the 1980s for the bulk of athletics programs to fully recover but approaching the year 2000 UVA was again one of the [most successful all-around sports programs](#Athletics) with NCAA national titles achieved in an array of different sports; by 2020, it had twice won the [Capital One Cup](/wiki/Capital_One_Cup_(college_sports) "Capital One Cup (college sports)") for overall athletics excellence in men's sports programs.

UVA established a [junior college](/wiki/Junior_college "Junior college") in 1954, known today as the [University of Virginia's College at Wise](/wiki/University_of_Virginia%27s_College_at_Wise "University of Virginia's College at Wise"). [George Mason University](/wiki/George_Mason_University "George Mason University") and [Mary Washington University](/wiki/Mary_Washington_University "Mary Washington University") used to similarly exist as UVA's [satellite campuses](/wiki/Satellite_campus "Satellite campus"), but those are now wholly independent universities no longer administered by the University of Virginia.

The [Academical Village](#Academical_Village) and nearby [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello "Monticello") became a joint [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1987. Simultaneously with [Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park](/wiki/Hawai%CA%BBi_Volcanoes_National_Park "Hawaiʻi Volcanoes National Park") and [Chaco Culture National Historical Park](/wiki/Chaco_Culture_National_Historical_Park "Chaco Culture National Historical Park"), they were the fifteenth, sixteenth, and seventeenth U.S. sites designated as culturally significant to the collective interests of global humanity, coming after the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") and [Yosemite National Park](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park") three years earlier. As such, UVA possesses the only U.S. collegiate grounds to be internationally protected by the [United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization](/wiki/United_Nations_Educational,_Scientific_and_Cultural_Organization "United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization") (UNESCO).

#### Integration, coeducation, and student dissent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Integration, coeducation, and student dissent")]

The University of Virginia first admitted a few selected women to graduate studies in the late 1890s and to certain programs such as nursing and education in the 1920s and 1930s.[[39]](#cite_note-Breaking_and_Making_Tradition-41) In 1944, [Mary Washington College](/wiki/University_of_Mary_Washington "University of Mary Washington") in [Fredericksburg, Virginia](/wiki/Fredericksburg,_Virginia "Fredericksburg, Virginia"), became the Women's Undergraduate Arts and Sciences Division of the University of Virginia. With this branch campus in Fredericksburg exclusively for women, UVA maintained its main campus in Charlottesville as near-exclusively for men, until a civil rights lawsuit in the 1960s forced it to commingle the sexes.[[40]](#cite_note-42) In 1970, the Charlottesville campus became fully co-educational, and in 1972 Mary Washington became an independent state university.[[41]](#cite_note-43) When the first female class arrived, 450 undergraduate women entered UVA, comprising 39 percent of undergraduates, while the number of men admitted remained constant. By 1999, women made up a 52 percent majority of the total student body.[[39]](#cite_note-Breaking_and_Making_Tradition-41)[[42]](#cite_note-enrollmentdata-44)

The university admitted its first black student when Gregory Swanson sued to gain entrance into the university's law school in 1950.[[43]](#cite_note-Road_to_Desegregation-45) Following his successful lawsuit, a handful of black graduate and professional students were admitted during the 1950s, though no black undergraduates were admitted until 1955, and UVA did not fully integrate until the 1960s.[[43]](#cite_note-Road_to_Desegregation-45) When Walter Ridley graduated with a doctorate in education, he was the first black person to graduate from UVA.[[43]](#cite_note-Road_to_Desegregation-45) UVA's Ridley Scholarship Fund is named in his honor.[[43]](#cite_note-Road_to_Desegregation-45)

The fight for integration and coeducation came to the foreground particularly in the late 1960s, leading up to the [May Strike of 1970](/wiki/Student_strike_of_1970 "Student strike of 1970"), in which students protested for higher black enrollment, equal access to UVA admission by undergraduate women, unionization of employees, and against the presence of armed university police and recruiters of government agencies such as the [CIA](/wiki/CIA "CIA") and [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") on Grounds.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Secretary_Kerry_Walks_With_UVA_President_Sullivan.jpg/330px-Secretary_Kerry_Walks_With_UVA_President_Sullivan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Kerry_Walks_With_UVA_President_Sullivan.jpg)

[President Sullivan](/wiki/Teresa_A._Sullivan "Teresa A. Sullivan") speaking with U.S. Secretary of State [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") in front of [the Rotunda](/wiki/The_Rotunda_(University_of_Virginia) "The Rotunda (University of Virginia)") in February 2013

Due to a continual decline in state funding for the university, today only 6 percent of its budget comes from the Commonwealth of Virginia.[[45]](#cite_note-CDstate-47) A Charter initiative was signed into law by then-[Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Virginia "Governor of Virginia") [Mark Warner](/wiki/Mark_Warner "Mark Warner") in 2005, negotiated with the university to have greater autonomy over its own affairs in exchange for accepting this [decline in financial support](#State_support).[[46]](#cite_note-Virginia_restructuring-48)[[47]](#cite_note-Virginia_restructuring_FAQ-49)

The university welcomed [Teresa A. Sullivan](/wiki/Teresa_A._Sullivan "Teresa A. Sullivan") as its first female president in 2010.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Just two years later its first woman rector, [Helen Dragas](/wiki/Helen_Dragas "Helen Dragas"), engineered a forced-resignation to remove President Sullivan from office.[[49]](#cite_note-Anatomy_of_a_Campus_Coup-51)[[50]](#cite_note-52) The attempted ouster elicited a faculty Senate vote of no confidence in Rector Dragas, and demands from student government for an explanation.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-StudentCouncilReaction-54) In the face of mounting pressure including alumni threats to cease contributions, and a mandate from then-Governor [Robert McDonnell](/wiki/Robert_McDonnell "Robert McDonnell") to resolve the issue or face removal of the entire Board of Visitors, the board unanimously reinstated President Sullivan.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) In 2013 and 2014, the board passed new bylaws that made it harder to remove a president and possible to remove a rector.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

In November 2014, the university suspended fraternity and sorority functions pending investigation of [an article](/wiki/A_Rape_on_Campus "A Rape on Campus") by *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* concerning an alleged rape story, which was later determined to be a [hoax](/wiki/Hoax "Hoax") after the story was confirmed to be false through investigation by *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*.[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-OTM-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61) The university nonetheless instituted new rules banning "pre-mixed drinks, punches or any other common source of alcohol" such as beer kegs and requiring "sober and lucid" fraternity members to monitor all parties.[[60]](#cite_note-The_Washington_Post_January_7_2015c-62) In April 2015, *Rolling Stone* fully retracted the article after the [Columbia School of Journalism](/wiki/Columbia_School_of_Journalism "Columbia School of Journalism") released a scathing and discrediting report on the "anatomy of a journalistic failure" by its author.[[61]](#cite_note-Somaiya_April-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64) Even before release of the Columbia University report, the *Rolling Stone* story was named the "Error of the Year" by the [Poynter Institute](/wiki/Poynter_Institute "Poynter Institute").[[63]](#cite_note-65) The UVA chapter of [Phi Kappa Psi](/wiki/Phi_Kappa_Psi "Phi Kappa Psi") settled a defamation suit against *Rolling Stone* and received $1.65 million.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

In August 2017, the night before the infamous [Unite the Right rally](/wiki/Unite_the_Right_rally "Unite the Right rally"), a group of non-student and mostly non-Virginian [white nationalists](/wiki/White_nationalism "White nationalism") marched on the university's [Lawn](/wiki/The_Lawn "The Lawn") bearing torches and chanting [antisemitic](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") and [Nazi](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") slogans after the city of [Charlottesville](/wiki/Charlottesville "Charlottesville") decided to remove all remaining [Confederate](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") statues from the city including one depicting [Robert E. Lee](/wiki/Robert_E._Lee "Robert E. Lee").[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) They were met by student counter-protesters near the statue of [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") in front of the Rotunda, where a fight broke out.

[James E. Ryan](/wiki/James_E._Ryan_(educator) "James E. Ryan (educator)"), a graduate of the [University of Virginia School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Law "University of Virginia School of Law"), became the university's ninth president in August 2018.[[67]](#cite_note-69) His first act upon his inauguration was to announce that in-state undergraduates from families making less than $80,000 per year would receive full scholarships covering tuition, and those from families making under $30,000 would also receive free room and board.[[68]](#cite_note-VAfree-70) Ryan was previously dean of the [Harvard School of Education](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Education "Harvard Graduate School of Education").

On the night of November 13, 2022, three students were killed and two others injured in [a shooting](/wiki/2022_University_of_Virginia_shooting "2022 University of Virginia shooting") on a [charter bus](/wiki/Bus#Private_charter "Bus") that was returning to the campus from a play for a class trip in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[69]](#cite_note-71) All three fatalities were current members of the [Virginia Cavaliers football](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_football "Virginia Cavaliers football") team[[70]](#cite_note-72) and the alleged shooter was briefly a member of the team during the 2018 season.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

In 2025, the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") called for President [Ryan](/wiki/James_E._Ryan_(educator) "James E. Ryan (educator)") to resign, citing university-wide efforts to stall and misrepresent the dismantling of the University's diversity, equity and inclusion policies.[[72]](#cite_note-74) Ryan announced his resignation on June 27, 2025.[[73]](#cite_note-guard-27jun2025-75) In October 2025, the university made a deal with the [Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump"), agreeing to "not engage in unlawful racial discrimination in its university programming, admissions, hiring or other activities".[[74]](#cite_note-guard-22oct2025-76)

On November 12, 2025, [Virginia governor](/wiki/Virginia_governor "Virginia governor")-elect [Abigail Spanberger](/wiki/Abigail_Spanberger "Abigail Spanberger") asked the University’s board of visitors to pause its search for a new president until she takes office, and could appoint members to the board. Governor [Glenn Youngkin](/wiki/Glenn_Youngkin "Glenn Youngkin") and conservative alumni criticized the move as political interference.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Grounds")]

This section is an excerpt from [Grounds of the University of Virginia](/wiki/Grounds_of_the_University_of_Virginia "Grounds of the University of Virginia").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Grounds_of_the_University_of_Virginia&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Untitled_%28Rotunda_-University_of_Virginia%29%2C_1912-1914%2C_Georgia_O%27Keeffe.jpg/250px-Untitled_%28Rotunda_-University_of_Virginia%29%2C_1912-1914%2C_Georgia_O%27Keeffe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Untitled_(Rotunda_-University_of_Virginia),_1912-1914,_Georgia_O%27Keeffe.jpg)

[The Rotunda](/wiki/The_Rotunda_(University_of_Virginia) "The Rotunda (University of Virginia)"), as painted by [American modernist](/wiki/American_modernism#Modernist_painting "American modernism") painter [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe") in the early 1910s when she was a [Summer Session student](/wiki/O%27Keeffe_at_the_University_of_Virginia,_1912%E2%80%931914 "O'Keeffe at the University of Virginia, 1912–1914")

The University of Virginia campus, referred to as [Grounds](/wiki/Grounds_of_the_University_of_Virginia "Grounds of the University of Virginia"),[[76]](#cite_note-Grounds_of_the_University_of_Virginia_grounds2-78) straddles the border between the [City of Charlottesville](/wiki/Charlottesville,_Virginia "Charlottesville, Virginia") and [Albemarle County](/wiki/Albemarle_County,_Virginia "Albemarle County, Virginia").[[77]](#cite_note-79) The university also maintains 562 acres north of the campus at [North Fork](#Research) and 2,913 acres southeast of the city at Morven Farm.[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)[[80]](#cite_note-Grounds_of_the_University_of_Virginia_WHS2-82) It also is in the process of building a campus in [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia") within [Fairfax, Virginia](/wiki/Fairfax,_Virginia "Fairfax, Virginia").[[81]](#cite_note-83)

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

[Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has the text of a [1911 *Encyclopædia Britannica*](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition") article about [circa 1911 organization of the university](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/1911_Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica/Virginia,_University_of "wikisource:1911 Encyclopædia Britannica/Virginia, University of").

The university has several affiliated centers including the [Rare Book School](/wiki/Rare_Book_School "Rare Book School"), headquarters of the [National Radio Astronomy Observatory](/wiki/National_Radio_Astronomy_Observatory "National Radio Astronomy Observatory"), [University of Virginia Center for Politics](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_Center_for_Politics "University of Virginia Center for Politics"), Weldon Cooper Center for Public Service, Sorensen Institute for Political Leadership, Institute for Advanced Studies in Culture, and [Miller Center of Public Affairs](/wiki/Miller_Center_of_Public_Affairs "Miller Center of Public Affairs"). The [Fralin Museum of Art](/wiki/Fralin_Museum_of_Art "Fralin Museum of Art") is dedicated to creating an environment where both the university community and the general public can study and learn from directly experiencing works of different art. The university has its own internal recruiting firm, the Executive Search Group and Strategic Resourcing. Since 2013, this department has been housed under the Office of the President.

In 2006, President Casteen announced an ambitious $3 billion capital campaign to be completed by December 2011.[[82]](#cite_note-84) During the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), President Sullivan missed the 2011 deadline, and extended it indefinitely.[[83]](#cite_note-85) The $3 billion goal would be met a year and a half later, which President Sullivan announced at graduation ceremonies in May 2013.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit), UVA's $1.4 billion academic budget is paid for primarily by tuition and fees (32%), research grants (23%), endowment and gifts (19%), and sales and services (12%).[[85]](#cite_note-UVAbudgetRevs-87) The university receives 10% of its academic funds through state appropriation from the Commonwealth of Virginia.[[85]](#cite_note-UVAbudgetRevs-87) For the overall (including non-academic) university budget of $2.6 billion, 45% comes from medical patient revenue.[[85]](#cite_note-UVAbudgetRevs-87) The Commonwealth contributes less than 6%.[[85]](#cite_note-UVAbudgetRevs-87)

UVA's endowment is among the highest among universities in the United States.[[86]](#cite_note-LargestEndowments-88) As of 2013, the University of Virginia was one of only two [public universities](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") in the United States that had a [Triple-A credit rating](/wiki/Bond_credit_rating "Bond credit rating") from all three major credit rating agencies.[[87]](#cite_note-89)

Although UVA is the flagship university of Virginia, state funding has decreased for several consecutive decades.[[45]](#cite_note-CDstate-47) Financial support from the state dropped by half from 12 percent of total revenue in 2001–02 to six percent in 2013–14.[[45]](#cite_note-CDstate-47) The portion of academic revenue coming from the state fell by even more in the same period, from 22 percent to just nine percent.[[45]](#cite_note-CDstate-47) This nominal support from the state, contributing just $154 million of UVA's $2.6 billion budget in 2012–13, has led President Sullivan and others to contemplate the partial privatization of the University of Virginia.[[88]](#cite_note-WPpriv-90) UVA's Darden School and Law School are already self-sufficient. [Hunter R. Rawlings III](/wiki/Hunter_R._Rawlings_III "Hunter R. Rawlings III"), President of the prominent [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") research group of universities, came to Charlottesville to make a speech to university faculty which included a statement about the proposal: "there's no possibility, as far as I can see, that any state will ever relinquish its ownership and governance of its public universities, much less of its flagship research university".[[88]](#cite_note-WPpriv-90) He encouraged university leaders to stop talking about privatization and instead push their state lawmakers to increase funding for higher education and research as a public good.[[88]](#cite_note-WPpriv-90)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Academics")]

The University of Virginia offers 48 [bachelor's degree](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") programs, 94 master's degree programs, 55 [doctoral degree](/wiki/Doctorate "Doctorate") programs, 6 educational specialist degree programs, and 2 first-professional degrees ([Medicine](/wiki/Doctor_of_Medicine "Doctor of Medicine") and [Law](/wiki/Juris_Doctor "Juris Doctor")). UVA does not bestow honorary degrees.[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-barton-mit-founding-93)

### Scholarships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Scholarships")]

The [Jefferson Scholarship](/wiki/Jefferson_Scholarship "Jefferson Scholarship") is a competitive merit scholarship. Around 30 scholars are selected annually from a direct application pool of 4,500 nominating schools, each able to nominate only one student. Covering all tuition, books, room and board, the scholarship also provides scholars finances for summer enrichment, independent research and study abroad.

[Echols Scholars](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_College_of_Arts_%26_Sciences#Echols_Scholars "University of Virginia College of Arts & Sciences") (College of Arts and Sciences) and [Rodman Scholars](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science#Rodman_Scholars_Program "University of Virginia School of Engineering and Applied Science") ([School of Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "University of Virginia School of Engineering and Applied Science")), which include 5% of undergraduate students, receive no financial benefits, but are entitled to special advisors, priority course registration, residence in designated dorms and fewer curricular constraints than other students have.[[92]](#cite_note-echols-benefits-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Shannon_Library%2C_UVA.jpg/330px-Shannon_Library%2C_UVA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shannon_Library,_UVA.jpg)

Shannon Library is home to 1.7 million books.[[93]](#cite_note-95) It is one of eleven libraries at UVA, and hosts one-third of the 1.9 million visitors to the system each year as of 2018.[[94]](#cite_note-96)

Full tuition scholarships are given to each in-state student from families earning under $100,000 per year.[[68]](#cite_note-VAfree-70) Each in-state student from families earning under $50,000 per year also receives free room and board.[[68]](#cite_note-VAfree-70) These scholarships are initiatives of President Ryan, who announced them upon his inauguration in 2018.[[68]](#cite_note-VAfree-70)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Research")]

The University of Virginia is the first and longest serving public member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") in the [American South](/wiki/American_South "American South"), attaining membership in 1904.[[95]](#cite_note-97) It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[96]](#cite_note-Carnegie-98)

According to the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), UVA spent $614 million on research and development in 2019, ranking it 44th in the nation and first in Virginia.[[97]](#cite_note-99) Built in 1996, North Fork (formerly the UVA Research Park[[98]](#cite_note-100)) is an extensive 3.7-million square foot, 562 acre [research park](/wiki/Research_park "Research park") nine miles north of UVA's North Grounds.[[99]](#cite_note-RPFuture-101)[[100]](#cite_note-ResearchPark-102) It houses the UVA Applied Research Institute as well as many private R&D efforts by such firms as [Battelle](/wiki/Battelle_Memorial_Institute "Battelle Memorial Institute"), The MITRE Corporation, Signature Science, and CACI.[[100]](#cite_note-ResearchPark-102)[[101]](#cite_note-103)

UVA is also home to globally recognized research on [hypersonic flight](/wiki/Hypersonic_flight "Hypersonic flight") for NASA and other organizations.[[102]](#cite_note-hypersonic-104) The [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"), National Science Foundation, and National Center for Hypersonic Combined Cycle Propulsion have each also granted UVA researchers millions in funding for the university's ongoing broad and deep research into ultra-high velocity flight.[[102]](#cite_note-hypersonic-104) Starting in 2015, a UVA team led by mechanical engineering professor Eric Loth began Department of Energy-funded research into an original design of [offshore wind turbines](/wiki/Offshore_wind_turbine "Offshore wind turbine") that would potentially dwarf the size and scope of any being produced or researched anywhere else.[[103]](#cite_note-WindTurbines-105) The innovative design inspired by [palm trees](/wiki/Palm_tree "Palm tree") led to Loth being named to a *[Popular Science](/wiki/Popular_Science "Popular Science")* list of "The Brilliant Minds Behind The New Energy Revolution".[[103]](#cite_note-WindTurbines-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107)

UVA was recognized by *[Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)")* as leading two of the top 10 scientific discoveries in the world in 2015.[[106]](#cite_note-Science2015-108) The first breakthrough was when [UVA School of Medicine](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Medicine "University of Virginia School of Medicine") researchers Jonathan Kipnis and Antoine Louveau discovered previously unknown vessels connecting the [human brain](/wiki/Human_brain "Human brain") directly to the [lymphatic system](/wiki/Lymphatic_system "Lymphatic system").[[106]](#cite_note-Science2015-108) The second breakthrough was when UVA psychology professor Brian Nosek examined the reproducibility of 100 psychology studies and found fewer than half could be reproduced.[[106]](#cite_note-Science2015-108) More than 270 researchers on five continents were involved, and twenty-two students and faculty from UVA were listed as co-authors on the scientific paper.[[106]](#cite_note-Science2015-108)

In the field of [astrophysics](/wiki/Astrophysics "Astrophysics"), the university is a member of a consortium engaged in the construction and operation of the [Large Binocular Telescope](/wiki/Large_Binocular_Telescope "Large Binocular Telescope") in the [Mount Graham International Observatory](/wiki/Mount_Graham_International_Observatory "Mount Graham International Observatory") of the [Pinaleno Mountains](/wiki/Pinaleno_Mountains "Pinaleno Mountains") of southeastern [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"). It is also a member of both the Astrophysical Research Consortium, which operates telescopes at [Apache Point Observatory](/wiki/Apache_Point_Observatory "Apache Point Observatory") in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), and the [Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy](/wiki/Association_of_Universities_for_Research_in_Astronomy "Association of Universities for Research in Astronomy") which operates the [National Optical Astronomy Observatory](/wiki/National_Optical_Astronomy_Observatory "National Optical Astronomy Observatory"), the [Gemini Observatory](/wiki/Gemini_Observatory "Gemini Observatory") and the [Space Telescope Science Institute](/wiki/Space_Telescope_Science_Institute "Space Telescope Science Institute"). The University of Virginia hosts the headquarters of the [National Radio Astronomy Observatory](/wiki/National_Radio_Astronomy_Observatory "National Radio Astronomy Observatory"), which operates the [Green Bank Telescope](/wiki/Green_Bank_Telescope "Green Bank Telescope") in [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") and the [Very Large Array](/wiki/Very_Large_Array "Very Large Array") radio telescope made famous in the [Carl Sagan](/wiki/Carl_Sagan "Carl Sagan") television documentary *[Cosmos](/wiki/Cosmos:_A_Personal_Voyage "Cosmos: A Personal Voyage")* and film *[Contact](/wiki/Contact_(1997_American_film) "Contact (1997 American film)")*. The North American [Atacama Large Millimeter Array](/wiki/Atacama_Large_Millimeter_Array "Atacama Large Millimeter Array") Science Center is also at the Charlottesville NRAO site. In 2019, researchers at NRAO co-authored a study documenting the discovery of a pair of giant [hourglass](/wiki/Hourglass "Hourglass") shaped [balloons](/wiki/Balloon "Balloon") emanating [radio waves](/wiki/Radio_wave "Radio wave") from the center of our [Milky Way galaxy](/wiki/Milky_Way_galaxy "Milky Way galaxy").[[107]](#cite_note-109)

For the undergraduate Class of 2027, the University of Virginia received a record 56,439 applications, admitting 16.2 percent.[[130]](#cite_note-Class27-132) The early action acceptance rate was 27 percent for in-state Virginians and 12 percent for out-of-state applicants.[[131]](#cite_note-Class27EA-133) The regular decision acceptance rate was 13 percent for in-state Virginians and 8 percent for out-of-state applicants.[[130]](#cite_note-Class27-132) UVA is required, by Virginia state law, to matriculate two-thirds of its undergraduate student body from its pool of in-state applicants;[[132]](#cite_note-2019div-134) it is barred, by Virginia state law, from giving admissions preference to the children of its alumni.[[133]](#cite_note-135) Approximately 40 percent of those admitted to UVA are non-white.[[132]](#cite_note-2019div-134) Matriculated students come from all 50 states and 147 foreign countries.[[134]](#cite_note-iaas-136)[[135]](#cite_note-CavAdmissionsHandbook-137) The university has seen steady increases to its applicant pool in recent decades, and the number of applications has more than tripled since the Class of 2008 received 15,094 applications.[[136]](#cite_note-138) Admission to the university is among the most [selective](/wiki/College_admissions_in_the_United_States "College admissions in the United States") in the United States among public universities.[[137]](#cite_note-MostSelectivePublics-139) As of 2014, 93 percent of admitted applicants ranked in the top 10 percent of their high school classes.[[135]](#cite_note-CavAdmissionsHandbook-137)[[138]](#cite_note-uva-2014stats-140) For the Class of 2027, some 1,167 students were accepted after having had their application fees waived as either low-income or first-generation college students.[[130]](#cite_note-Class27-132)

Ryan (educator)") announced at his inauguration in fall 2018 that in-state students from families earning less than $80,000 a year will receive full tuition scholarships.[[68]](#cite_note-VAfree-70) Those from families earning less than $30,000 will also receive free room and board.[[68]](#cite_note-VAfree-70) The university already met 100 percent of demonstrated need for all admitted undergraduate students, making it one of only two public universities in the U.S. to reach this level of financial aid for its students.[[140]](#cite_note-AccessUVa-142)[[141]](#cite_note-143) For 2014, the university ranked fourth overall by the *[Princeton Review](/wiki/Princeton_Review "Princeton Review")* for "Great Financial Aid".[[142]](#cite_note-144) In 2008 the Center for College Affordability and Productivity named UVA the top value among all national public colleges and universities; and in 2009, UVA was again named the "No. 1 Best Value" among public universities in the United States in a separate ranking by *[USA TODAY](/wiki/USA_TODAY "USA TODAY")* and the *Princeton Review.*[[143]](#cite_note-145)[[144]](#cite_note-146)[[145]](#cite_note-147) *[Kiplinger](/wiki/Kiplinger "Kiplinger")* in 2014 ranked UVA second out of the top 100 best-value public colleges and universities in the nation.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

Graduate and professional school admissions are also highly selective. As of 2024, the average [LSAT](/wiki/Law_School_Admissions_Test "Law School Admissions Test") score was 172 at the [School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Law "University of Virginia School of Law"), while at the [Darden School of Business](/wiki/Darden_School_of_Business "Darden School of Business") the average [GMAT](/wiki/Graduate_Management_Admissions_Test "Graduate Management Admissions Test") score was 718.[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-150)

Student life at the University of Virginia is marked by a number of unique traditions. The campus of the university is referred to as the "Grounds". Freshmen, sophomores, juniors, and seniors are instead called first-, second-, third-, and fourth-years to reflect Jefferson's belief that learning is a lifelong process, rather than one to be completed within four years.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/William_Faulkner_1954_%282%29_%28photo_by_Carl_van_Vechten%29.jpg/250px-William_Faulkner_1954_%282%29_%28photo_by_Carl_van_Vechten%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Faulkner_1954_(2)_(photo_by_Carl_van_Vechten).jpg)

[William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner") once lived among the students of UVA after winning the [Nobel Prize for Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_for_Literature "Nobel Prize for Literature"), and bequeathed most of his papers to Shannon Library.

### Student-faculty interaction and connections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Student-faculty interaction and connections")]

Professors are traditionally addressed as "Mr." or "Ms." at UVA instead of "Doctor" (although medical doctors are the one exception) in deference to Jefferson's desire to have an equality of ideas.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

First-year students in the College of Arts & Sciences have the opportunity to take four Engagements, two per semester, with the goal of students expanding their thinking.[[151]](#cite_note-155)

Select faculty live at [Brown College at Monroe Hill](/wiki/Brown_College_at_Monroe_Hill "Brown College at Monroe Hill"), [Hereford College](/wiki/Hereford_College "Hereford College"), [International Residential College](/wiki/International_Residential_College "International Residential College"), and in Pavilions on [the Lawn](/wiki/The_Lawn "The Lawn"). This living arrangement gives more opportunities for professors to invite students to lunches and dinners, which regularly happens, and creates chances for impromptu meetings and interactions between faculty and students around Grounds.

Reflecting this close student-faculty interaction at UVA, it welcomed Nobel Laureate [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner") to a position as "Writer-in-Residence" in 1957.[[152]](#cite_note-Blotner-156) He had no teaching responsibilities, and was paid merely to live among the students and write. He was badly injured in a horse riding accident in 1959, and did not return to the state before his death in 1962.[[152]](#cite_note-Blotner-156) Faulkner then bequeathed the majority of his papers to Shannon Library, giving UVA the largest Faulkner archives in the world.[[153]](#cite_note-157)

### Global citizenship initiatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Global citizenship initiatives")]

UVA was previously the academic sponsor for [Semester at Sea](/wiki/Semester_at_Sea "Semester at Sea"), a multi-country study abroad program conducted on a cruise ship.

The University of Virginia received the 2015 Paul Simon Award for Comprehensive Internationalization, by the Association of International Educators.[[154]](#cite_note-Simon-158) This award confirms the university's success and commitment in educating its students on a global scale as well as nationally.[[154]](#cite_note-Simon-158)

### Student leadership opportunities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Student leadership opportunities")]

There are a number of UVA undergraduate leadership opportunities that are offered in addition to the standard student government or fraternity and sorority positions found at many other universities. They include UVA's [secret societies and debating societies](#Secret_societies), the [student-run honor committees](#Honor_system), and the chance to be recognized as a fourth-year student at the pinnacle of student leadership by being asked to live on [the Lawn](/wiki/The_Lawn "The Lawn").

The [Frank Batten School of Leadership and Public Policy](/wiki/Frank_Batten_School_of_Leadership_and_Public_Policy "Frank Batten School of Leadership and Public Policy"), established in 2007, expands on these unique student leadership opportunities to study Leadership itself as a cross-disciplinary subject of focus and is closely aligned with many of the university's schools, including the [Architecture](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Architecture "University of Virginia School of Architecture"), [Education](/wiki/Curry_School_of_Education "Curry School of Education"), [Engineering](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science "University of Virginia School of Engineering and Applied Science"), [Law](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Law "University of Virginia School of Law"), [Medical](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Medicine "University of Virginia School of Medicine"), and [Darden](/wiki/Darden_Graduate_School_of_Business_Administration "Darden Graduate School of Business Administration") schools, as well as with programs in politics, economics, and applied ethics.

The University of Virginia has a long history of student activists who formed radical environmental, religious, and political groups to champion various social changes.[[155]](#cite_note-159) An especially intense period of student activism occurred in the 1970s during the May Days strikes against the Vietnam War.[[156]](#cite_note-160) More recently, the [School of Education and Human Development](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Education_and_Human_Development "University of Virginia School of Education and Human Development") and its Youth-Nex Center held a national conference in 2019 to promote student activism at UVA and beyond.[[157]](#cite_note-161)

### Secret societies

Main article: [Secret Societies at the University of Virginia](/wiki/Secret_Societies_at_the_University_of_Virginia "Secret Societies at the University of Virginia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/IMPsecretsociety.jpg/250px-IMPsecretsociety.jpg)](/wiki/File:IMPsecretsociety.jpg)
