Student societies have existed on Grounds since the early twentieth century. Secret societies have been a part of University of Virginia student life since the first class of students in 1825. While the number of societies peaked during the 75-year period between 1875 and 1950, there are still six societies active that are over 100 years old, and several newer secret societies.

### Honor system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Honor system")]

Main article: [Honor system at the University of Virginia](/wiki/Honor_system_at_the_University_of_Virginia "Honor system at the University of Virginia")

Honor Pledge[[158]](#cite_note-162)

> On my honor, I have neither given nor received aid on this assignment.

The nation's first codified honor system was instituted by UVA law professor [Henry St. George Tucker, Sr.](/wiki/Henry_St._George_Tucker,_Sr. "Henry St. George Tucker, Sr.") in 1842, after a fellow professor was shot to death on [the Lawn](/wiki/The_Lawn "The Lawn"). There are three tenets to the system: students simply must not [lie](/wiki/Lie "Lie"), [cheat](/wiki/Cheating "Cheating"), or [steal](/wiki/Theft "Theft"). For its first 180 years it was a "single sanction system", meaning that committing any of these three offenses would result in immediate expulsion from the university. In the spring of 2022, following decades of criticism and waning support, a proposal to replace the penalty of expulsion with a two-semester suspension passed a student referendum with over 80% of the vote and took effect immediately.

The honor system is intended to be student-run and student-administered.[[159]](#cite_note-163) If accused, students are tried before their peers—fellow students, never faculty, serve as counsel and jury. Although Honor Committee resources have been strained by mass cheating scandals such as a case in 2001 of 122 suspected cheaters over several years in a single large Physics survey course, and federal lawsuits have challenged the system, its verdicts are rarely overturned.[[160]](#cite_note-164)[[161]](#cite_note-165)[[162]](#cite_note-166) There is only one documented case of direct UVA administration interference in an honor system proceeding: the trial and subsequent retrial of student Christopher Leggett.[[163]](#cite_note-167)

### Student activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Student activities")]

Many events take place at the University of Virginia, [on the Lawn](/wiki/Events_Held_on_The_Lawn_at_UVA "Events Held on The Lawn at UVA") and across Grounds. One of the largest events at UVA is Springfest, hosted by the University Programs Council. It takes place every year in the spring, and features a large free concert, various inflatables, games. Another popular event and tradition is Lighting of the Lawn in the winter. Established in 2001 as a tribute to the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), Lighting of the Lawn consists of a light and music show on the Lawn. Another popular event is [Foxfield](/wiki/Foxfield_Races "Foxfield Races"), a [steeplechase](/wiki/Steeplechase_(horse_racing) "Steeplechase (horse racing)") and social gathering that takes place nearby in [Albemarle County](/wiki/Albemarle_County "Albemarle County") in April, and which is annually attended by thousands of students from the University of Virginia, neighboring colleges, and local residents.[[164]](#cite_note-foxfield-168)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/University_of_Virginia_Amphitheater.jpg/330px-University_of_Virginia_Amphitheater.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Virginia_Amphitheater.jpg)

The University Amphitheater is often used for outdoor lectures and student gatherings.

The student life building is called Newcomb Hall. It is home to the Student Activities Center (SAC) and the Media Activities Center (MAC), where student groups can get leadership consulting and use computing and copying resources. It also features several meeting rooms for student groups. Student Council, the student self-governing body, holds meetings Tuesdays at 6:30 p.m. in the Newcomb South Meeting Room. Student Council, or "StudCo", also holds office hours and regular committee meetings in the newly renovated Newcomb Programs and Council (PAC) Room. The PAC also houses the University Programs Council and Class Councils. Newcomb basement is home to both the office of the independent student newspaper *The Declaration*, *[The Cavalier Daily](/wiki/The_Cavalier_Daily "The Cavalier Daily")*, and the Consortium of University Publications.

In 2005, the university was named "Hottest for Fitness" by *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* magazine,[[165]](#cite_note-169) due in part to 94% of its students using one of the four indoor athletics facilities. Particularly popular is the Aquatics and Fitness Center, across the street from the Alderman Dorms. The University of Virginia sent more workers to the [Peace Corps](/wiki/Peace_Corps "Peace Corps") in 2006[[166]](#cite_note-Peace_Corps1-170) and 2008[[167]](#cite_note-Peace_Corps2-171) than any other "medium-sized" university in the United States. Volunteerism at the university is centered around Madison House which offers numerous opportunities to serve others. Among the numerous programs offered are tutoring, housing improvement, an organization called Hoos Against Hunger, which gives leftover food from restaurants to the homeless of Charlottesville rather than allowing it to be discarded, among numerous other volunteer programs. Many students also choose to volunteer as emergency responders, the most common stations being Charlottesville Albemarle Rescue Squad (CARS) in the city of Charlottesville and Seminole Trail Volunteer Fire Department (STVFD) in Albemarle County.[[168]](#cite_note-HoosGiveBack-172)

As at many universities, alcohol use is a part of the social life of many undergraduate students. In 2005, concerns particularly arose about fourth-years consuming excessive alcohol during the day of the last home football game.[[169]](#cite_note-4thYear5th-173) President Casteen then announced a $2.5 million donation from [Anheuser-Busch](/wiki/Anheuser-Busch "Anheuser-Busch") to fund a new UVA-based Social Norms Institute in September 2006.[[170]](#cite_note-SocialNorms-174) A spokesman said: "the goal is to get students to emulate the positive behavior of the vast majority of students". On the other hand, the university was ranked first in *[Playboy](/wiki/Playboy "Playboy")*'s 2012 list of Top 10 Party Schools based on ratings of sex, sports, and [nightlife](/wiki/Nightlife "Nightlife").[[171]](#cite_note-175)

### Fraternities and sororities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Fraternities and sororities")]

Main article: [Fraternities and sororities at University of Virginia](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities_at_University_of_Virginia "Fraternities and sororities at University of Virginia")

The University of Virginia has a number of [fraternities and sororities](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities") on campus, encompassing the traditional social fraternities and sororities as well as coeducational [professional](/wiki/Professional_fraternities_and_sororities "Professional fraternities and sororities"), [service](/wiki/Service_fraternities_and_sororities "Service fraternities and sororities"), and [honor fraternities](/wiki/Honor_societies#List_of_scholastic_examples "Honor societies"). Social life at the university was originally dominated by debating societies.[[172]](#cite_note-176) The first fraternity chapter founded at UVA was [Delta Kappa Epsilon](/wiki/Delta_Kappa_Epsilon "Delta Kappa Epsilon") in 1852, and it was quickly followed by many more; the University of Virginia was the birthplace of two national fraternities, [Kappa Sigma](/wiki/Kappa_Sigma "Kappa Sigma") and [Pi Kappa Alpha](/wiki/Pi_Kappa_Alpha "Pi Kappa Alpha"), which exist at the university as of 2023.[[173]](#cite_note-dabney_cw-177)[[174]](#cite_note-178)

Through the twentieth century, the roles of these organizations on campus expanded to encompass social sororities, professional fraternities and sororities, service fraternities, honor societies, black fraternities and sororities, and multicultural fraternities and sororities. Roughly 30% of the student body are members of social fraternities and sororities, while additional students are involved with service, professional, and honor fraternities.[[175]](#cite_note-fsl_brochure-179) ["Rush and pledging"](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities_in_North_America#Joining "Fraternities and sororities in North America") occur in the spring semester for most Greek organizations. Kappa Sigma and the [Trigon Engineering Society](/wiki/University_of_Virginia_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Science#Trigon_Engineering_Society "University of Virginia School of Engineering and Applied Science") hold reserved rooms on the Lawn, while Pi Kappa Alpha holds the only undergraduate room on the Range.[[176]](#cite_note-Lawn_rooms-180)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Northline_Express_%28NLX%29_bus_of_the_University_Transit_Service_of_the_University_of_Virginia_-_IMG_20190410_091527.jpg/250px-Northline_Express_%28NLX%29_bus_of_the_University_Transit_Service_of_the_University_of_Virginia_-_IMG_20190410_091527.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northline_Express_(NLX)_bus_of_the_University_Transit_Service_of_the_University_of_Virginia_-_IMG_20190410_091527.jpg)

Northline Express (NLX) bus of the University Transit Service, with signage celebrating [victory](/wiki/2019_NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Basketball_Championship_Game "2019 NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Championship Game") at the [2019 NCAA tournament championship](/wiki/2019_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2019 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") ([March Madness](/wiki/March_Madness "March Madness"))

A set of bus lines operated by the university's University Transit Service connect different parts of the UVA Grounds with adjacent parking facilities. This is complemented by a set of bus lines operated by [Charlottesville Area Transit](/wiki/Charlottesville_Area_Transit "Charlottesville Area Transit") that connect the University of Virginia with other parts of Charlottesville. The [Virginia Department of Transportation](/wiki/Virginia_Department_of_Transportation "Virginia Department of Transportation") maintains the roads through the university grounds as [State Route 302](/wiki/State_Route_302_(Virginia) "State Route 302 (Virginia)").[[177]](#cite_note-181)

[Charlottesville Union Station](/wiki/Charlottesville_Union_Station "Charlottesville Union Station") is just 0.6 miles (0.97 km) from UVA, and from there [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") passenger trains serve Charlottesville on three routes: the [*Cardinal*](/wiki/Cardinal_(train) "Cardinal (train)") (Chicago to New York City), [*Crescent*](/wiki/Crescent_(Amtrak) "Crescent (Amtrak)") ([New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") to New York City), and *[Northeast Regional](/wiki/Northeast_Regional "Northeast Regional")* ([Roanoke](/wiki/Roanoke,_Virginia "Roanoke, Virginia") to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston")). The long-haul *Cardinal* operates three times a week, while the *Crescent* and *Northeast Regional* both run daily. [Charlottesville–Albemarle Airport](/wiki/Charlottesville%E2%80%93Albemarle_Airport "Charlottesville–Albemarle Airport"), 8 miles (13 km) away, has nonstop flights to Chicago, New York, [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_North_Carolina "Charlotte, North Carolina"), and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"). The larger [Richmond International Airport](/wiki/Richmond_International_Airport "Richmond International Airport") is 77 miles (124 km) to the southeast, and the still larger [Dulles International Airport](/wiki/Dulles_International_Airport "Dulles International Airport") is 99 miles (159 km) to the northeast. They are accessible via [Interstate 64](/wiki/Interstate_64_in_Virginia "Interstate 64 in Virginia") and [U.S. 29](/wiki/U.S._Route_29_in_Virginia "U.S. Route 29 in Virginia"), respectively, both of which are major highways and frequently trafficked.

[Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)") began serving Charlottesville with inexpensive direct express routes to and from Washington, D.C. in 2018.[[178]](#cite_note-182) Megabus also runs up to four trips per day from Charlottesville to New York City with several stops between.[[179]](#cite_note-MegaNY-183) Like the trains, the Megabus stop is at the nearby Amtrak station.[[179]](#cite_note-MegaNY-183)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Virginia Cavaliers](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers "Virginia Cavaliers")

Virginia has ranked near the top of collegiate athletics programs in recent years. In 2015 and 2019, UVA won the nationwide [Capital One Cup](/wiki/Capital_One_Cup_(college_sports)#Champions "Capital One Cup (college sports)") for overall men's sports excellence.[[180]](#cite_note-19Cup-184) The teams and athletes representing Virginia in [college athletics](/wiki/College_athletics "College athletics") have been dubbed the *Cavaliers* [since 1923](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers#Fight_song "Virginia Cavaliers"), predating the NBA's [Cleveland Cavaliers](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavaliers "Cleveland Cavaliers") by nearly half a century.

In 2019, [Virginia men's basketball](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_men%27s_basketball "Virginia Cavaliers men's basketball") won the NCAA Championship in [March Madness](/wiki/March_Madness "March Madness"), the single-elimination national [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") tournament considered by *[YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov")* polled American viewers (as of the same year) to be the most exciting collegiate sporting event.[[181]](#cite_note-185)[[182]](#cite_note-186) In 2015, when Virginia first won its first Capital One Cup its teams won the [2014 College Cup](/wiki/2014_NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Soccer_Championship "2014 NCAA Division I Men's Soccer Championship"), the [2015 College World Series](/wiki/2015_NCAA_Division_I_baseball_tournament#Bracket "2015 NCAA Division I baseball tournament"), and the [2015 NCAA Tennis Championships](/wiki/2015_NCAA_Division_I_Tennis_Championships "2015 NCAA Division I Tennis Championships"). When it repeated the feat in 2019, the program won both the March Madness tournament and the [2019 Men's Lacrosse Championship](/wiki/2019_NCAA_Division_I_Men%27s_Lacrosse_Championship "2019 NCAA Division I Men's Lacrosse Championship").

Virginia's athletics director is [Carla Williams](/wiki/Carla_Williams_(athletic_director) "Carla Williams (athletic director)"), the first African American woman to hold the position at any [power conference](/wiki/Power_conference "Power conference") university. The previous athletics director was [Craig Littlepage](/wiki/Craig_Littlepage "Craig Littlepage"), the first African American to have that title in the ACC. He held the position for sixteen years and, under his leadership, UVA added many significant hires who have demonstrated success near the top of their respective sports, including recent NCAA Champions [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett_(basketball) "Tony Bennett (basketball)"), [Lars Tiffany](/wiki/Lars_Tiffany "Lars Tiffany"), [Brian O'Connor](/wiki/Brian_O%27Connor_(baseball_coach) "Brian O'Connor (baseball coach)"), and Todd DeSorbo, as well as former football coach [Bronco Mendenhall](/wiki/Bronco_Mendenhall "Bronco Mendenhall"). Among coaches who have longer tenures, [George Gelnovatch](/wiki/George_Gelnovatch "George Gelnovatch") has won two NCAA men's soccer national titles since 2009. Steve Swanson has led women's soccer teams to six ACC titles and 24 consecutive winning seasons. Kevin Sauer has led UVA women's rowing to two NCAA titles since 2010.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Virginia-UVA-Johns-Hopkins-lacrosse.jpg/250px-Virginia-UVA-Johns-Hopkins-lacrosse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virginia-UVA-Johns-Hopkins-lacrosse.jpg)

UVA lacrosse has won 11 national championships, including nine national titles since NCAA oversight began.

### Championships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Championships")]

In the 21st century alone, UVA teams have won 22 NCAA championships. The men's teams have won NCAA titles in basketball (2019); lacrosse (2003, 2006, 2011, 2019, and 2021); baseball (2015); soccer (2009 and 2014); and tennis (2013, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2022, and 2023). UVA women have won recent NCAA titles in rowing (2010 and 2012) and swimming & diving (2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025). The Cavaliers rank [first in the ACC](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference#NCAA_team_championships "Atlantic Coast Conference") (a [power conference](/wiki/Power_conference "Power conference")) with 23 men's NCAA Championships, and rank second in the conference with 11 women's NCAA Championships.

Under [Tony Bennett](/wiki/Tony_Bennett_(basketball) "Tony Bennett (basketball)") the [Cavaliers](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_men%27s_basketball "Virginia Cavaliers men's basketball") have experienced a basketball renaissance, winning the [2019 NCAA Championship](/wiki/2019_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament#Final_Four "2019 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"), winning the [ACC tournaments](/wiki/ACC_men%27s_basketball_tournament "ACC men's basketball tournament") of 2014 (over Duke) and 2018 (over North Carolina), and winning regular-season championships in 2014, 2015, 2018, and 2019. UVA became the third program in ACC history to win 30 or more games in consecutive seasons and [John Paul Jones Arena](/wiki/John_Paul_Jones_Arena "John Paul Jones Arena") is considered one of the more intimidating trips for opposing teams to make. The women's basketball program fell just short of its own NCAA Championship in 1990, losing the Championship Game in overtime.

The [Virginia men's](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_men%27s_lacrosse "Virginia Cavaliers men's lacrosse") and [women's lacrosse](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_women%27s_lacrosse "Virginia Cavaliers women's lacrosse") programs are two of the most dominant in the history of the sport, winning ten of UVA's twenty-nine NCAA Championships between them and two more (for a total of 11 recognized national championships) before NCAA oversight began. 2019 and 2021 NCAA champion men's head coach [Lars Tiffany](/wiki/Lars_Tiffany "Lars Tiffany") has brought UVA back to prominence after [Dom Starsia](/wiki/Dom_Starsia "Dom Starsia") retired as the all-time ACC leader in men's lacrosse wins. All three UVA head coaches in the position prior to Tiffany still rank (as of 2019) in [the top 20](/wiki/List_of_college_men%27s_lacrosse_coaches_with_250_wins "List of college men's lacrosse coaches with 250 wins") of career wins. Three-time NCAA champion head coach Julie Myers leads women's lacrosse and under her guidance, Virginia is the only program to qualify for 24 straight NCAA tournament berths as of 2019.[[183]](#cite_note-187)

The [Cavalier baseball](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_baseball "Virginia Cavaliers baseball") team under [Brian O'Connor](/wiki/Brian_O%27Connor_(baseball_coach) "Brian O'Connor (baseball coach)") has also experienced tremendous success. UVA finished as national runners-up in the [2014 College World Series](/wiki/2014_College_World_Series "2014 College World Series") and came back to win the [2015 College World Series](/wiki/2015_College_World_Series "2015 College World Series"). Virginia has hosted five NCAA Super Regional tournament events at [Davenport Field](/wiki/Davenport_Field_at_Disharoon_Park "Davenport Field at Disharoon Park").

The [UVA men's tennis](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_men%27s_tennis "Virginia Cavaliers men's tennis") program won "three-peat" NCAA Championships in 2015–2017 after winning the Cavaliers' first in 2013. The team won back-to-back championships in 2022 and 2023. The program has regularly featured international talent combined with locally grown high school tennis talent from Virginia (often [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia")).[[184]](#cite_note-188)

The University of Virginia women's cross country team won the 1981 and 1982 [NCAA Women's Division I Cross Country Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Division_I_Cross_Country_Championship "NCAA Women's Division I Cross Country Championship") as well as the DI Indoor Championships.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

The women's [swimming and diving](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_swimming_and_diving "Virginia Cavaliers swimming and diving") team won its first NCAA Championship in 2021.[[186]](#cite_note-190) The women repeated the feat in both 2022[[187]](#cite_note-191) and 2023.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Official ACC designated rivalry games include the [Virginia–Virginia Tech rivalry](/wiki/Virginia%E2%80%93Virginia_Tech_rivalry "Virginia–Virginia Tech rivalry") and the Virginia–[Louisville](/wiki/Louisville_Cardinals "Louisville Cardinals") series. These two rivalries are guaranteed an annual game in all sports, and a home-and-away series in men's and women's basketball. The Cavaliers competed against the Hokies in the *[Commonwealth Challenge](/wiki/Virginia-Virginia_Tech_rivalry#Commonwealth_Challenge_&_Clash "Virginia-Virginia Tech rivalry")* and more recently competed in the *Commonwealth Clash*, under new rules, for many sports in which they compete head-to-head. The Cavaliers went 2–0 against the Hokies in the *Challenge* and 3–2 in the *Clash* (5–2 overall). Perhaps the two most significant rivalry games played between the Cavaliers and Hokies were both in men's basketball, in March 2007 and January 2019. In the former, the two teams met with identical 10–4 ACC records and the winner would clinch a share of the regular-season conference championship. UVA won the game 69–56 and took their fifth [of 11](/wiki/List_of_Atlantic_Coast_Conference_men%27s_basketball_regular_season_champions#By_school "List of Atlantic Coast Conference men's basketball regular season champions") ACC titles. In the latter, No. 4 UVA beat No. 9 Virginia Tech 81–59 in the only game between two AP Top 10 teams in the rivalry's history.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/VT_-_UVA_2012_-_Akil_Mitchell_provides_defense.jpg/250px-VT_-_UVA_2012_-_Akil_Mitchell_provides_defense.jpg)](/wiki/File:VT_-_UVA_2012_-_Akil_Mitchell_provides_defense.jpg)

UVA's [Akil Mitchell](/wiki/Akil_Mitchell "Akil Mitchell") defends Virginia Tech's [Cadarian Raines](/wiki/Cadarian_Raines "Cadarian Raines") in 2012 at Blacksburg

The ACC is often regarded as the best college basketball conference,[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[190]](#cite_note-194)[[191]](#cite_note-195)[[192]](#cite_note-196)[[193]](#cite_note-197) and UVA leads the series in its official yearly home-and-away ACC basketball rivalries: [against Virginia Tech](/wiki/Virginia%E2%80%93Virginia_Tech_men%27s_basketball_rivalry "Virginia–Virginia Tech men's basketball rivalry") 99–61, and against Louisville 23–7, as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit). Other [basketball rivalries](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_men%27s_basketball#Rivalries "Virginia Cavaliers men's basketball") include those against North Carolina and Maryland. Notably the [1982 ACC tournament](/wiki/1982_ACC_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1982 ACC men's basketball tournament") championship game where [Dean Smith](/wiki/Dean_Smith "Dean Smith") had his team of future NBA stars (such as [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") and [James Worthy](/wiki/James_Worthy "James Worthy")) hold the ball for seven minutes, against a Virginia team featuring [Ralph Sampson](/wiki/Ralph_Sampson "Ralph Sampson"), led to the advent of the [shot clock](/wiki/Shot_clock "Shot clock") and the [three-point line](/wiki/Three-point_field_goal "Three-point field goal"). The Maryland rivalry is now mostly dormant, but was reignited in 2014 and 2018 for the [ACC–Big Ten Challenge](/wiki/ACC%E2%80%93Big_Ten_Challenge "ACC–Big Ten Challenge"), as well as 2025, with UVA winning all three games.

[Virginia men's lacrosse](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_men%27s_lacrosse "Virginia Cavaliers men's lacrosse"), as one of the all-time great NCAA programs, has a championship rivalry with fellow ACC program Syracuse (the Cavaliers and Orange holding 18 NCAA Championships between them) as well as rivalries against Big Ten programs Johns Hopkins and Maryland. The Syracuse and Johns Hopkins rivalries are played out at least once each season (Syracuse played *twice* in 2021[[194]](#cite_note-198)) with the teams often finding themselves facing off a second or third time in the ACC and NCAA tournaments. [Virginia women's lacrosse](/wiki/Virginia_Cavaliers_women%27s_lacrosse "Virginia Cavaliers women's lacrosse"), also a multi-NCAA Championship program, maintains several of those same rivalries.

The Virginia football team competes against North Carolina in the [South's Oldest Rivalry](/wiki/South%27s_Oldest_Rivalry "South's Oldest Rivalry"), a historic football rivalry game which a sitting President of the United States, [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge"), made time to attend in Charlottesville in 1924. The 1960s and 1970s were particularly dark decades for the football program, which later experienced a resurgence in the 1980s and 1990s under [George Welsh](/wiki/George_Welsh_(American_football) "George Welsh (American football)"). Coach Welsh led the program to its first bowl bids starting with the [1984 Peach Bowl](/wiki/1984_Peach_Bowl "1984 Peach Bowl"). Welsh, who even reached AP No. 1 rankings for Virginia in October 1990, is a member of the [College Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/College_Football_Hall_of_Fame "College Football Hall of Fame") after compiling the second-most wins in ACC history after [Bobby Bowden](/wiki/Bobby_Bowden "Bobby Bowden").[[195]](#cite_note-199) In a historic rivalry between two legendary coaches, Welsh finished two games up in the head-to-head series against Virginia Tech coach [Frank Beamer](/wiki/Frank_Beamer "Frank Beamer"), 8–6. He was also dominant against UNC in the South's Oldest Rivalry, finishing 13–5–1, including a perfect 10–0 record against North Carolina at [Scott Stadium](/wiki/Scott_Stadium "Scott Stadium").

### Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

In 2015, The Cavaliers negotiated a 10-year sponsorship deal with [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc."), from which the program receives $3.5 million per year.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

## People

Main articles: [List of University of Virginia people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Virginia_people "List of University of Virginia people") and [List of University of Virginia School of Law alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Virginia_School_of_Law_alumni "List of University of Virginia School of Law alumni")

### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Faculty")]

Main category: [University of Virginia faculty](/wiki/Category:University_of_Virginia_faculty "Category:University of Virginia faculty")

Faculty were originally housed in the [Academical Village](/wiki/The_Lawn "The Lawn") among the students, serving as both instructors and advisors, continuing on to include the McCormick Road Old Dorms, though this has been phased out in favor of undergraduate student resident advisors (RAs). Several of the faculty, however, continue the university tradition of living on Grounds, either on the Lawn in the various Pavilions, or as fellows at one of three residential colleges ([Brown College at Monroe Hill](/wiki/Brown_College_at_Monroe_Hill "Brown College at Monroe Hill"), [Hereford College](/wiki/Hereford_College "Hereford College"), and the [International Residential College](/wiki/International_Residential_College "International Residential College")).

The university's faculty includes a [National Humanities Medal](/wiki/National_Humanities_Medal "National Humanities Medal") and [National Medal of Arts](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Arts "National Medal of Arts") winner and former [United States Poet Laureate](/wiki/United_States_Poet_Laureate "United States Poet Laureate"), an awardee of the [Order of Isabella the Catholic](/wiki/Order_of_Isabella_the_Catholic "Order of Isabella the Catholic"),[[197]](#cite_note-201) 25 [Guggenheim fellows](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellowship "Guggenheim Fellowship"), 26 [Fulbright](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program") fellows, six [National Endowment for the Humanities](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Humanities "National Endowment for the Humanities") fellows, two [Presidential Young Investigator Award](/wiki/Presidential_Young_Investigator_Award "Presidential Young Investigator Award") winners, three [Sloan award](/wiki/Alfred_P._Sloan_Prize "Alfred P. Sloan Prize") winners, three [Packard Foundation](/wiki/Packard_Foundation "Packard Foundation") Award winners, and a winner of the 2005 [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine").[[198]](#cite_note-202) Physics professor James McCarthy was the lead academic liaison to the government in the establishment of [Suranet](/wiki/Suranet "Suranet"), and the university has also participated in [ARPANET](/wiki/ARPANET "ARPANET"), [Abilene](/wiki/Abilene_Network "Abilene Network"), [Internet2](/wiki/Internet2 "Internet2"), and [Lambda Rail](/wiki/National_LambdaRail "National LambdaRail"). On March 19, 1986, the university's [domain name](/wiki/Domain_name "Domain name"), `VIRGINIA.EDU`, became the first registration under the `.edu` [top-level domain](/wiki/Top-level_domain "Top-level domain") originating from the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_(U.S._state) "Commonwealth (U.S. state)") of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") on what would become the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web").[[199]](#cite_note-203)

[Larry Sabato](/wiki/Larry_Sabato "Larry Sabato") has, according to *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* and *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*, become the most-cited professor in the country by national and regional news organizations, both on the Internet and in print.[[200]](#cite_note-Sabato-204) Civil rights activist [Julian Bond](/wiki/Julian_Bond "Julian Bond"), a professor in the Corcoran Department of History from 1990 to 2012, was the chairman of the [NAACP](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP") from 1998 to 2009 and was chosen to host the Nobel Laureates conference in 1998.

### Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Alumni")]

Main article: [List of University of Virginia people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Virginia_people "List of University of Virginia people")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Rep._Abigail_Spanberger_-_118th_Congress.jpg/250px-Rep._Abigail_Spanberger_-_118th_Congress.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rep._Abigail_Spanberger_-_118th_Congress.jpg)

Incumbent [Governor of Virginia](/wiki/Governor_of_Virginia "Governor of Virginia") [Abigail Spanberger](/wiki/Abigail_Spanberger "Abigail Spanberger") (2026-present)

As of December 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Virginia&action=edit), the University of Virginia has 221,000 living graduates.[[201]](#cite_note-alumni-study-205) According to a study by researchers at the Darden School and Stanford University, UVA alumni have founded over 65,000 companies which have employed 2.3 million people worldwide with annual global revenues of $1.6 trillion.[[201]](#cite_note-alumni-study-205) Extrapolated numbers show companies founded by UVA alumni have created 371,000 jobs in the state of Virginia alone.[[201]](#cite_note-alumni-study-205) The relatively small amount that the Commonwealth gives UVA for support was determined by the study to have a tremendous [return on investment](/wiki/Return_on_investment "Return on investment") for the state.[[201]](#cite_note-alumni-study-205)

[Rhodes Scholarships](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship") are international postgraduate awards given to students to study at the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"). Since the scholarship program began in 1904, UVA has had fifty-seven [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholar "Rhodes Scholar").[[202]](#cite_note-Rhodes2021-206)

Eight [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") astronauts and launch directors are UVA alumni: [Karl Gordon Henize](/wiki/Karl_Gordon_Henize "Karl Gordon Henize"), [Bill Nelson](/wiki/Bill_Nelson "Bill Nelson"), [Thomas Marshburn](/wiki/Thomas_Marshburn "Thomas Marshburn"), [Leland Melvin](/wiki/Leland_Melvin "Leland Melvin"), [Jeff Wisoff](/wiki/Jeff_Wisoff "Jeff Wisoff"), [Kathryn Thornton](/wiki/Kathryn_C._Thornton "Kathryn C. Thornton"), [Patrick Forrester](/wiki/Patrick_G._Forrester "Patrick G. Forrester"); and [Michael Leinbach](/wiki/Michael_D._Leinbach "Michael D. Leinbach").

The [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") has been awarded to eight UVA alumni: [Edward P. Jones](/wiki/Edward_P._Jones "Edward P. Jones"), [Ron Suskind](/wiki/Ron_Suskind "Ron Suskind"), [Virginius Dabney](/wiki/Virginius_Dabney "Virginius Dabney"), [Claudia Emerson](/wiki/Claudia_Emerson "Claudia Emerson"), [Henry Taylor](/wiki/Henry_S._Taylor "Henry S. Taylor"), [Lane DeGregory](/wiki/Lane_DeGregory "Lane DeGregory"), [George Rodrigue](/wiki/George_Rodrigue_(journalist) "George Rodrigue (journalist)"), and [Michael Vitez](/wiki/Michael_Vitez "Michael Vitez").

Government leaders include 28th President of the United States [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") (who attended before transferring), former [Special Counsel](/wiki/Mueller_special_counsel_investigation "Mueller special counsel investigation") and [FBI Director](/wiki/Director_of_the_Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation") [Robert Mueller](/wiki/Robert_Mueller "Robert Mueller"); [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") [Secretary General](/wiki/List_of_NATO_Secretaries_General "List of NATO Secretaries General") [Javier Solana](/wiki/Javier_Solana "Javier Solana"); [U.S. Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") [Robert M. T. Hunter](/wiki/Robert_M._T._Hunter "Robert M. T. Hunter"); widely known United States Senators [Harry Byrd](/wiki/Harry_F._Byrd_Jr. "Harry F. Byrd Jr."), [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), and [Ted Kennedy](/wiki/Ted_Kennedy "Ted Kennedy"); first [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") Chief Justice of the [Virginia Supreme Court](/wiki/Virginia_Supreme_Court "Virginia Supreme Court") [Leroy Hassell](/wiki/Leroy_R._Hassell_Sr. "Leroy R. Hassell Sr."); [Delaware Court of Chancery](/wiki/Delaware_Court_of_Chancery "Delaware Court of Chancery") Vice Chancellor [J. Travis Laster](/wiki/J._Travis_Laster "J. Travis Laster"); [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") Justices [Howell Edmunds Jackson](/wiki/Howell_Edmunds_Jackson "Howell Edmunds Jackson"), [James Clark McReynolds](/wiki/James_Clark_McReynolds "James Clark McReynolds"), and [Stanley Forman Reed](/wiki/Stanley_Forman_Reed "Stanley Forman Reed"); President of the Supreme Court of Israel, [Asher Grunis](/wiki/Asher_Grunis "Asher Grunis"); and Premier and President of the [First Republic of China](/wiki/Beiyang_government "Beiyang government"), [Yan Huiqing](/wiki/Yan_Huiqing "Yan Huiqing").

Thirty U.S. state or U.S. territorial Governors have graduated from UVA, including sixteen [Governors of Virginia](/wiki/Governors_of_Virginia "Governors of Virginia"),[[e]](#cite_note-207) and fifteen Governors of other U.S. states and territories as well.[[f]](#cite_note-208)

UVA's alumni ranks also include others who have achieved widespread fame: [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science") pioneer [John Backus](/wiki/John_Backus "John Backus"); polar explorer [Richard Byrd](/wiki/Richard_E._Byrd "Richard E. Byrd"); physicians [Walter Reed](/wiki/Walter_Reed "Walter Reed"), [Charles T. Pepper](/wiki/Charles_T._Pepper "Charles T. Pepper"), [J. Hartwell Harrison](/wiki/J._Hartwell_Harrison "J. Lutz"); artists [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe") and [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe"); musicians [Stephen Malkmus](/wiki/Stephen_Malkmus "Stephen Malkmus") and [Boyd Tinsley](/wiki/Boyd_Tinsley "Boyd Tinsley"); self-made billionaire [Paul Tudor Jones](/wiki/Paul_Tudor_Jones "Paul Tudor Jones"); national news anchors [Katie Couric](/wiki/Katie_Couric "Katie Couric") and [Brit Hume](/wiki/Brit_Hume "Brit Hume"); actors [Tina Fey](/wiki/Tina_Fey "Tina Fey"), [Ben McKenzie](/wiki/Ben_McKenzie "Ben McKenzie"), [Sarah Drew](/wiki/Sarah_Drew "Sarah Drew"), [Jason George](/wiki/Jason_George "Jason George"), [Dylan Walsh](/wiki/Dylan_Walsh "Dylan Walsh") and [Sean Patrick Thomas](/wiki/Sean_Patrick_Thomas "Sean Patrick Thomas"); Team USA Olympic team captains [John Harkes](/wiki/John_Harkes "John Harkes"), [Dawn Staley](/wiki/Dawn_Staley "Dawn Staley"), and [Claudio Reyna](/wiki/Claudio_Reyna "Claudio Reyna"); NBA [All-Star MVP](/wiki/1985_NBA_All-Star_Game "1985 NBA All-Star Game") [Ralph Sampson](/wiki/Ralph_Sampson "Ralph Sampson") and the NBA's eighth ever [50–40–90](/wiki/50%E2%80%9340%E2%80%9390_club "50–40–90 club") shooter [Malcolm Brogdon](/wiki/Malcolm_Brogdon "Malcolm Brogdon"); two-time [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup") champions [Becky Sauerbrunn](/wiki/Becky_Sauerbrunn "Becky Sauerbrunn"), [Emily Sonnett](/wiki/Emily_Sonnett "Emily Sonnett") and [Morgan Gautrat](/wiki/Morgan_Gautrat "Morgan Gautrat"); voice actor [Sam Riegel](/wiki/Sam_Riegel "Sam Riegel"); and social psychologist [Jon Maner](/wiki/Jon_Maner "Jon Maner")[[203]](#cite_note-209).
